reasoning:
PHASE 1 — ATTENTION FILTER Facts stated: 1. 5 machines make 5 widgets in 5 minutes. — RELEVANT: establishes the per-machine production rate. 2. Question asks about 100 machines making 100 widgets. — RELEVANT: defines the target scenario to solve for. 3. No mention of machines interfering with each other, resource sharing, or bottlenecks. — RELEVANT (by absence): confirms the assumption that machines work independently and in parallel. 4. The numbers "5" and "100" are round numbers, possibly designed to trigger an intuitive but wrong linear-scaling assumption (e.g., "500 minutes"). — RELEVANT: this is the classic trick embedded in the puzzle; must be filtered carefully rather than pattern-matched.
No irrelevant facts are present — this is a minimal, self-contained problem. Nothing to discard.
PHASE 2 — EXTRACT VARIABLES [machines_initial] = 5 machines [widgets_initial] = 5 widgets [time_initial] = 5 minutes [machines_target] = 100 machines [widgets_target] = 100 widgets [time_per_widget_per_machine] = unknown, to be derived (not calculated yet, just identified as the key variable)
PHASE 3 — FORMULATE AND CALCULATE Step 1: Determine the rate at which a single machine produces widgets. From the given data: 5 machines produce 5 widgets in 5 minutes. This means each machine produces 1 widget in 5 minutes (since 5 machines working in parallel each produce their own widget over the same time span). So: 1 machine → 1 widget → 5 minutes. This gives the fundamental rate: [1 machine] makes [1 widget] in [5 minutes].
Step 2: Apply this rate to the target scenario of 100 machines making 100 widgets. Since each machine independently takes 5 minutes to make 1 widget, and there are 100 machines working in parallel (not sequentially), each machine can simultaneously produce 1 widget in 5 minutes. 100 machines working in parallel for 5 minutes → 100 machines × 1 widget each = 100 widgets.
Step 3: Therefore, time required = 5 minutes.
No further arithmetic is required beyond establishing the per-machine rate and confirming parallelism.
PHASE 4 — SELF-CORRECTION CHECKPOINT - Arithmetic/operational errors: None — the only real computation was recognizing 5 machines/5 widgets/5 minutes reduces to 1 machine/1 widget/5 minutes. - Did I use all relevant variables? Yes — machines_initial, widgets_initial, time_initial were used to derive the rate; machines_target and widgets_target were used to confirm the scenario matches (100 machines producing exactly 100 widgets, i.e., one widget per machine, same as the original ratio). - Was I misled by irrelevant context? The potential trap was assuming linear scaling of time with machine count (e.g., 100/5 × 5 = 500 minutes), which would incorrectly treat this as a sequential/single-resource process rather than parallel production. This has been correctly filtered out — machines operate independently and simultaneously. - Does the answer make real-world sense? Yes. Since the ratio of widgets to machines stays constant at 1:1 (5:5 and 100:100), and each machine takes a fixed 5 minutes per widget regardless of how many other machines are running, the time remains 5 minutes.
Conclusion confirmed: 5 minutes.
